chunk_summary: Emergency management of acute septic arthritis involves joint aspiration, drainage, and imaging to prevent cartilage damage, especially in critical joints like the hip.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical presentation and urgency of acute septic arthritis.
Learn the indications and techniques for joint aspiration and drainage.
Recognize imaging modalities and their role in diagnosis and management.
Identify when orthopedic involvement is necessary for septic arthritis cases.
key_facts: Acute septic arthritis can cause rapid cartilage damage, particularly in the hip.
Synovial fluid analysis includes cell count, Gram stain, bacterial culture, and molecular testing.
Urgent operative drainage is often required for the hip due to elevated intracapsular pressure threatening femoral head perfusion.
Needle aspiration and lavage may suffice for peripheral joints like the knee, ankle, and shoulder if fluid is thin and clinical improvement is rapid.
Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is recommended when fever persists, inflammation is severe, or concurrent osteomyelitis is suspected.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: The hip usually requires urgent operative drainage or repeat aspiration because elevated intracapsular pressure threatens femoral head perfusion. (Indian Academy of Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
document_key_topics: Acute septic arthritis diagnosis and management
Imaging and orthopedic involvement in septic arthritis
